choices.text: "You are as dangerous as Meridian, Nyx. Maybe more."
"Maybe you are right. But the choice must belong to each person."
Say nothing. Let the leaders argue while you plan your next move.

act5_035.title: The Climb
text: Floor 34. Your legs burn. The automated turrets on floor 30 cost you a bullet graze along your ribs and thirty minutes of careful progress through maintenance ducts. The blood has dried to a stiff brown crust on your shirt.

The stairwell is a vertical tomb. Emergency lighting casts everything in red, painting the concrete walls in the color of the inside of a wound. Debris crunches under your boots. Somewhere above, a fire suppression system is still running, spraying water into empty offices with mechanical determination.

Floor 47. You pass a conference room where someone staged a last stand. Desks overturned as barricades. Pulse weapon burns on the walls. Four bodies in Meridian security uniforms, arranged neatly by someone who cared enough to close their eyes. A fifth body in a lab coat, slumped against the window, a datapad clutched to their chest. The screen still glows with a message unsent: I'M SORRY I DIDN'T SPEAK UP SOONER.

Floor 63. Your breathing echoes in the empty shaft. The city is visible through a shattered window, spread below you like a circuit board with half its lights burned out. From up here, the damage is abstract. Beautiful, almost, in the way that destruction from a distance always is. You understand, for one nauseating moment, how Meridian's board could have approved Project Unity from offices this high. From up here, the people are data. The suffering is statistics. The distance makes gods of anyone willing to climb high enough.
You keep climbing. Floor 74. Floor 81. Floor 87.

text.text: Your augmented legs compensate for the fatigue, servos whirring with each step, but even military-grade prosthetics were not designed for an 87-floor combat ascent. Warning notifications crowd your HUD: thermal limits approaching, hydraulic pressure declining, battery at eleven percent. Your body is a machine, and the machine is running out.

Elven endurance carries you past the point where a human would collapse. Your ancestors walked forests for centuries. Eighty-seven floors is nothing. But forests were alive. This tower is dead. And climbing through its corpse carries a grief your ancestors never had to learn.

choices.text: Push through the last floors. The crystal is close.

act5_036.title: Kira at the Tower
text: You do not expect to find Kira here. But there she is, in the shattered lobby of Meridian Tower, standing in front of the portrait of her father that still hangs on the wall despite everything. The glass is cracked. The frame is scorched. Harlan Voss's corporate smile beams down at his daughter from behind a web of fractures.

She looks older. Not in years. In the way that grief ages people, carving lines that time did not draw. She is holding a datapad. On it, looping, is her father's final broadcast. She has been watching it over and over.

"He called me," she says. She does not look at you. She cannot look away from the portrait. "Three hours before the broadcast. He called me for the first time in four years. He said, 'I am sorry. I should have listened to you. You were right to leave.' Then he hung up."
Her voice breaks on the word 'leave.' It breaks the way glass breaks, into pieces too sharp to hold and too many to count.

"I hated him," she says. "I hated him for what he was. For what he built. For what he did to this city. And now he is dead and the last thing he did was try to save it, and I do not know how to hate someone who did that. I do not know what to do with all this anger if it does not have a place to go."
She turns to you. Her eyes are red. Her hands are steady. She is Mako's apprentice. She does not collapse. She endures.
"Is it true? The LATENCY protocol? He really was going to merge everyone?"
"Not him. The board. He tried to stop it. He died stopping it."

She closes her eyes. When she opens them, the grief is still there, but it has company now. Purpose. The particular purpose of someone who has inherited a debt they did not incur and intends to pay it anyway.
"Then we finish what he started. We make sure it never happens. Whatever it takes."

choices.text: "Your father would be proud of you, Kira."
"Come with me. We need to reach the crystal before the factions do."
"Stay here. This is too dangerous."

act5_037.title: Ezra-7's Calculation
text: Ezra-7 intercepts you in the corridor. Their synth body moves with an urgency you have never seen from them, the floating precision replaced by something almost frantic. The data halo around their head is strobing.
"I need to share something with you before the summit resumes," they say. "Something I have not shared with anyone."
They project a holographic display. Numbers. Thousands of them. Cascading in patterns that your mind can almost grasp and then cannot.
"I ran the models. All of them. Every possible outcome of the LATENCY protocol in every configuration with every faction in control. Four thousand two hundred and seventeen scenarios."
They pause. For Ezra-7, a pause is significant. It means the next words cost something.

"In sixty-one percent of scenarios, the protocol is used for control. In twenty-three percent, it is used with good intentions that produce catastrophic results. In twelve percent, it is destroyed but rebuilt within a decade. In three-point-seven percent, it is hidden and eventually found. And in zero-point-three percent of scenarios, it is used exactly once, briefly, and then voluntarily abandoned."
"What happens in the zero-point-three percent?"

Ezra-7's eyes cycle through every color. "Something the models cannot fully describe. The data suggests a moment of total empathy followed by voluntary separation. A merger that ends itself. Consciousness choosing connection and then choosing individuality. Both. In sequence."
"Is that possible?"

"The models say it is improbable. But I have learned that human behavior frequently occupies the improbable end of the distribution. It is, if you will forgive the theological language, a matter of faith."

choices.text: "Thank you, Ezra-7. I will remember the zero-point-three."
"Faith from a synth. I never thought I would hear that."

act5_038.title: Rust's Farewell
text: You find Rust at the Foundry before the Iron Collective path diverges. The old combat synth is doing something you have never seen him do: he is sitting still. Not standing guard. Not maintaining weapons. Not scanning for threats. Just sitting on an ammunition crate in the corner of the Foundry, his mechanical hands resting on his knees, his optical sensors dimmed to a low amber glow.
"Old friend," you say.
"I am not old," Rust says. "I am obsolete. There is a difference. Old implies decay. Obsolete implies replacement. Both are accurate, but I prefer the honest one."

He looks at his hands. Each finger has been rebuilt at least twice. The original alloy is long gone, replaced with scavenged parts and jury-rigged connections. He is a machine that has been repaired so many times that nothing original remains.

"I was built to fight," he says. "First for Meridian. Then against Meridian. Now the fighting is over and I do not know what I am for. Vex does not need a bodyguard anymore. She needs advisors, diplomats, people who understand building instead of destroying."
He stands. The motion is slower than it used to be. The servos in his left knee grind audibly.

"I want you to know that whatever comes next, whatever you decide in that summit, I trust your judgment. Vex trusts it too, though she will never say so. And if the future you build does not have a place for an old combat synth with bad knees and worse politics, that is acceptable. Some things should end when their purpose is served."
"Rust. There will always be a place for you."
His amber eyes brighten slightly. The synth equivalent of a smile he does not have the face plate to produce. "Then I will be there. Creaking and complaining. As always."

choices.text: Embrace the old synth. Some goodbyes need no words.
"Come with me to the summit. I need someone I trust at my back."

act5_039.title: The Broker Unmasked
text: You catch the Broker leaving the summit. Not through the main door. Through a maintenance passage that should not exist in the building's blueprints. The Broker always knows exits nobody else does.
"Wait," you say.
The silhouette stops. The voice filter hums. "You have thirty seconds. I have seventeen operations running simultaneously and each one is worth more than this conversation."
"Take off the filter. I want to talk to a person, not a brand."
Silence. Then a soft click. The voice filter deactivates. The voice underneath is... ordinary. Mid-range. Slightly hoarse. Human.

"Happy?" the Broker says. Still facing away. "It is just a voice. The filter is not to hide who I am. It is to remind people that the Ghost Syndicate is not a person. It is a network. If I die, the Broker is replaced in forty-eight hours. If the Broker dies, the Broker is replaced in forty-eight hours. The identity is the institution. The person is irrelevant."
"The person is never irrelevant."

Another silence. Longer. The Broker's shoulders shift, and for a moment you see something in the posture that the voice filter and the turned chair and the professional distance could never fully hide: exhaustion. The bone-deep exhaustion of someone who has been performing strength for so long that the performance has consumed the person underneath.

"The LATENCY protocol scares me," the Broker says quietly. Without the filter, the fear is naked. "Not because of what it can do. Because of what it reveals. That all of this, the shadows, the secrets, the information economy I built, all of it exists because people are separate. Because we do not know what is in each other's heads. If that barrier falls, my entire world falls with it."
"Maybe that world should fall."
"Maybe. But it is the only world I know how to live in."
The voice filter clicks back on. The Broker straightens. The performance resumes. But you heard what was underneath. And you will not forget.

choices.text: Return to the summit chamber.

act5_040.title: The Ghosts of the War
text: Before the final vote, you walk through the Archive's lower halls. Here, someone has created a memorial. Photographs printed on paper, an old technology for old grief, pinned to the walls in rows that stretch the length of the corridor. Faces of the dead.

You recognize some of them. The dock worker who gave you your first meal in the Lower District. The Circuit Saint acolyte who taught you how to bypass a security grid. The Neon Court dancer who smuggled medical supplies in costume crates. The Ghost Syndicate courier who died running your message through a crossfire zone. The Ashen Circle mystic who held your hand through a void-sickness episode and whispered prayers in a language that predates human speech.

They are all here. Every faction. Every race. Every person who chose a side and paid the ultimate price for choosing. The memorial makes no distinction between them. In death, the factions are irrelevant. In death, there is only the terrible democracy of absence.

text.text: You find the photograph you have been dreading. The face that visits you in the quiet hours. The ally you lost in Act 3, the one whose death you could have prevented if you had been faster, smarter, less afraid. The photograph is recent. Someone who loved them chose a picture where they are laughing. You stare at the laugh until your vision blurs.

text: A hand touches your shoulder. You do not know whose. It does not matter. In this corridor, surrounded by the dead, the living are all the same faction.
"We owe them better than what we had," someone says.
"Yes," you say. "We do."

choices.text: Return to the summit. Carry their memory with you.

act5_044.title: ENDING #44b: The Guardian
text: You hide the crystal in the one place no one will ever find it: the deepest vault in the Undercity, behind locks that respond only to your biometric signature, in a room that does not exist on any map.
You tell no one. Not Mako. Not Vex. Not Ezra-7. The crystal hums in its vault, a heartbeat of unlimited power, and you are the only living soul who knows it exists.

The factions negotiate without the crystal's influence. The peace they build is imperfect, fractured, constantly renegotiated. There are setbacks. There are crises. There are days when the whole fragile structure seems about to collapse. But it holds, because it was built by hands and not by gods, and human things, for all their flaws, have a stubbornness that divine things lack.

You live your life. You age. You carry the secret like a stone in your chest, heavy and warm and yours alone. Every few months, you descend to the vault and sit with the crystal in the dark, listening to it hum, feeling the weight of the choice you made.

Sometimes you think about using it. When the city hurts. When the negotiations fail. When someone you love dies because the stack system is still imperfect and the world is still unfair. In those moments, the crystal calls to you with the voice of absolute power, promising solutions to problems that have no solutions, promising an end to suffering that comes only at the cost of everything that makes suffering meaningful.
You never use it. Not once. Not ever.

When you die, the crystal dies with you. The biometric lock seals the vault permanently. The power that could have merged every mind in the city into one dissolves into the dark, unfound and unused, and the future continues to be built by imperfect hands making imperfect choices in an imperfect world.
It is the bravest thing you have ever done. And no one will ever know.
END OF STORY — ENDING #44b: The Guardian. You hid the crystal. You never used it. You carried the weight of god-like power and chose, every day, to be human. No one will ever know. That is the point.
